riddles.riddle: I have a face and two hands, but no arms or legs. What am I?
riddles.explanation: Clocks have a 'face' (the front with numbers) and 'hands' (the pointers that move), but they don't have real arms or legs like a person.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: It tells you what time it is. It hangs on the wall.

riddles.riddle: What has keys but cannot open any locks?
riddles.explanation: Pianos have black and white 'keys' that you press to make music, but they are not metal keys for opening doors.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You play music with it. It has black and white keys.

riddles.riddle: I have a tail and a head, but no body. What am I?
riddles.explanation: Coins have a 'head' (the front picture of a person) and a 'tail' (the back side), but they don't have a body like an animal.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You use it to buy candy or gum. It's made of metal.

riddles.riddle: What gets wetter as it dries?
riddles.explanation: When you use a towel to dry something (like your body or a spill), the towel itself gets wet from the water it absorbs.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You use it after a bath or shower to dry yourself.

riddles.riddle: What has to be broken before you can use it?
riddles.explanation: You have to crack or break the hard shell of an egg to get to the yolk and white inside so you can cook and eat it.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: It comes from a chicken. You eat it for breakfast.

riddles.riddle: I have cities, but no houses. I have mountains, but no trees. I have water, but no fish. What am I?
riddles.explanation: Maps show pictures of places like cities, mountains, and water, but they are not real - they are just drawings on paper.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: It helps you find your way when you are lost.

riddles.riddle: What has a thumb and four fingers but is not alive?
riddles.explanation: Gloves have a part for your thumb and four parts for your fingers, but gloves are made of fabric or leather, not living skin.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You wear it on your hand in winter to keep warm.

riddles.riddle: What can you catch but never throw?
riddles.explanation: You can 'catch a cold' (get sick with a virus), but you cannot throw a cold like a ball because it's an illness.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: It makes you sneeze, cough, and feel yucky.

riddles.riddle: What building has the most stories?
riddles.explanation: 'Stories' can mean tales in books OR floors in a building. Libraries have thousands of storybooks, so they have the most 'stories'.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: Where do you go to borrow books to read?

riddles.riddle: What is full of holes but still holds water?
riddles.explanation: Sponges have many tiny holes (pores), but they soak up and hold water inside those holes when you squeeze them.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You use it to clean dishes or take a bath.

riddles.riddle: I am light as a feather, yet the strongest person cannot hold me for five minutes. What am I?
riddles.explanation: Breath is very light (you can't even feel it), but no one can hold their breath (stop breathing) for more than a few minutes.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You do this all day without thinking. You need it to live.

riddles.riddle: What belongs to you but other people use it more than you do?
riddles.explanation: Your name belongs to you, but other people say your name more often than you say it yourself. You rarely say your own name!
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: People call you by it. Friends and teachers say it every day.

riddles.riddle: What has words but never speaks?
riddles.explanation: Books have many words printed on their pages, but a book cannot talk out loud like a person can.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You read it before bed. It has a cover and pages.

riddles.riddle: What can you hold without ever touching?
riddles.explanation: You can 'hold your breath' (stop breathing), but you never physically touch your breath because it's air inside your lungs.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You do this when you go underwater in a swimming pool.

riddles.riddle: What has one eye but cannot see?
riddles.explanation: A sewing needle has an 'eye' (the hole where thread goes through), but it doesn't have a real eye that can see.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You use it to sew clothes or fix a button.

riddles.riddle: What has a neck but no head?
riddles.explanation: Bottles have a 'neck' (the narrow part at the top), but they don't have a head like a person or animal.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You drink from it. It holds water or soda.

riddles.riddle: What gets bigger the more you take from it?
riddles.explanation: When you take dirt or material away from a hole, the hole gets larger and larger. Taking away makes it bigger!
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You dig it in the ground. Animals live in it.

riddles.riddle: What is so delicate that saying its name breaks it?
riddles.explanation: Silence is the absence of sound. If you speak (say the word 'silence'), you break the silence by making noise.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: The library is full of it. Shhhh!

riddles.riddle: What has teeth but cannot eat?
riddles.explanation: Combs have 'teeth' (the little spikes that go through your hair), but they cannot eat food like real teeth.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You use it on your hair after a bath.

riddles.riddle: What has a ring but no finger?
riddles.explanation: Telephones 'ring' (make a ringing sound), but they don't have a metal ring for a finger like jewelry.
riddles.kid_friendly_hint: You use it to call Grandma. It goes 'brrrring brrrring'!
